item.title: New publication in Nature Communications
item.description:
Our latest paper titled The role of hydration in the removal of glyphosate (GLY) and aminomethylphosphonic acid (AMPA) by nanofiltration membranes has been accepted for publication in Nature Communications journal (2025 IF of 15.7).

This paper marks the fourth and final Ph.D. chapter of Dr. Phuong Bich Trinh, an IAMT-KIT colleague who also comes from Vietnam. Phuong carried out all the experiments and painstalking sample analysis with the LC/MS/MS. I contributed to the experimental design and sharpened up discussions.

What the paper is about

We discuss how hydration governs the nanofiltration of small and charged pesticide micropollutants – glyphosate, the most widely used pesticide, and its metabolite AMPA.

To isolate the removal associated with the shredding of the water shells (dielectric exclusion), six nanofiltration membranes with varying surface chemistry and molecular weight cut-off are experimented under different operating conditions.

At low pH, where the pesticides have close to neutral charge and charge (Donnan) exclusion is minimized, we observe clear evidence that the strongly bound water molecules hinder the pesticide transport.

The strength of the water shells is further characterized by collaborators who are experts in infra-red spectroscopy and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations.

Indeed, our results underscore the importance of hydration in micropollutant nanofiltration and offer insights for enhancing water purification membranes.

Link to the publication

item.title: New publication in Environmental Science & Technology
item.description:
Our paper titled Interference of the real water matrix with micropollutant removal via advanced filtration: Insights from the Goreangab Reservoir in Namibia has been accepted for publication in Enviromental Science & Technology (EST) journal (2025 IF of 11.3).

Dr. Martyna Krajewska – my IAMT-KIT colleague – performed most of the analyses and took charge in writing this exciting paper. I contributed to developing filtration protocols, carried out a number of filtration experiments, performed LC-OCD, and revised some of the texts.

What the paper is about

This paper provides a comparison between nine different pressure-driven and advanced membrane processes for removing micropollutants in the Goreangab Reservoir water.

Once a source for the world’s first direct potable reuse system (Windhoek), the reservoir has experienced significant water quality deterioration. We detect 28 types of micropollutants, including benzotriazoles, pesticides, pharmaceuticals and personal care products, endocrine disruptors, and PFAS).

The treatment conditions are tailored for a particular micropollutant type – steroid hormone 17β-estradiol (E2) – which is spiked in the reservoir water at a concentration of 100 ng/L.

Membrane photocatalysis, membrane electrocatalysis, and combined ultrafiltration–adsorption are comparable to nanofiltration in E2 removal performance.

However, energy requirements, susceptibility to fouling, long-term performance, and upscaling potential are practical limitations that are discussed in the paper.

Link to the publication

item.title: MSA Best Oral Presentation Award, IMSTEC 2025
item.description:
Pleased to share that I received the Membrane Society of Australasia (MSA) Best Oral Presentation Award at the IMSTEC 2025 Conference, Gold Coast, Australia. The conference was an amazing event to meet new people who are on the other side of the globe and learn about their works.

My presentation titled Collisions between singlet oxygen and steroid hormone micropollutants in photocatalytic membrane pores was based on a recently published paper in Chemical Engineering Journal (2024 IF of 13.2).

In the presentation, I discussed how the century-old collision theory could be adapted to predict the rate of micropollutant removal by photocatalytic membrane reactors.

Link to the publication

item.title: New publication in Chemical Engineering Journal
item.description:
Our paper titled Steroid hormone micropollutant removal from membrane bioreactor effluents using single-walled carbon nanotube composite nanofiber membranes has been accepted for publication in Chemical Engineering Journal (2025 IF of 13.5).

Han-Ya Lin, my colleague and a final-year Ph.D. student at IAMT-KIT, performed rigorous experiments and wrote most of the paper. I contributed in several aspects (time keeping, organic matter analysis, and text revision).

What the paper is about

This paper highlights the challenges of introducing novel technologies (such as dynamic adsorption by carbon nanotubes) into practical water treatment applications, particularly micropollutant removal.

Beyond the safety concerns, we identify key limitations: i) strong competition from organic matter, which reduces adsorption performance, and ii) high specificity towards certain micropollutants, limiting performance for others in a complex water matrix.

These findings suggest that, in its current form, carbon nanotube adsorption is nowhere near replacing the “big names” − ozonation or powdered activated carbon adsorption − for micropollutant removal.

In a field where novel materials or processes are frequently promoted, this paper discusses why it is important to take a step back and perform a critical practicality assessment.

Link to the publication

item.title: About This Website
item.description:
This is my personal website, where I feature my research directions, publications, and CV.

I also set up a blog where I share my achievements (paper publications, awarded grants, etc.), some thoughts on my academic journey, and other stuff.

The design of this website

The website is simplistic and fit for its simple purposes. I keep the main sections (Research, Publications, and CV) updated from time to time. Blog posts are time-stamped and organised based on tags and categories. No search bar, because I think it is a distraction.

Due to privacy concerns, I do not track visits or enable comments using tools such as Google Analytics or Disqus. If needs be in the future, I may opt for some privacy-focused alternatives.

If you want to follow my posts, please subscribe to the website’s RSS (which is an archaic content format) at the bottom of each page, next to the copyright information.

The website is hosted by Github and built from Hugo. The website layouts are adapted from the Hugo-Xmin theme by Yihui Xie, with some slight tuning of fonts and colours.

What you will NOT find on this website

  • Unpublished scientific contents in any form (manuscript sections, internal reports, discussions, ideas, etc.);
  • Donation requests;
  • My political views (I detest discussing politics in scientific settings).
